title: Dhalpuri roti
subtitle: Trinidadian split pea stuffed roti
excerpt: Soft, large flatbreads filled with a dry paste of boiled and ground yellow split peas seasoned with cumin and garlic, then cooked on a tawa or flat pan until blistered and pliable. Dhalpuri is the most celebrated roti in Trinidad — more complex than sada roti, with the ground split pea filling adding a faint nuttiness and body to each tear. The whole process takes about two hours but produces a flatbread unlike anything in any other tradition.
sourceNotes: Dhalpuri roti developed in Trinidad from the culinary traditions of indentured Indian workers who arrived after Emancipation. The technique of grinding boiled split peas and using them as a dry filling inside a flatbread is specific to the Trinidadian adaptation — distinct from the stuffed parathas of the Indian subcontinent. The name comes from 'dhal' (split peas) and 'puri' (fried bread), although dhalpuri itself is cooked dry, not fried.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked roti between sheets of baking paper. Reheat in a dry pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dhal filling can be made a day ahead and kept in the fridge. The dough is best made fresh.

content.text: The split pea filling must be completely dry before using. After boiling and draining the peas, spread them on a tray and allow any steam to escape before processing. A wet filling tears the dough when you roll it. The ground peas should be the consistency of fine damp sand — not a paste.

Rolling dhalpuri requires light hands. The filling wants to push out from the sides as you roll — work gently from the centre outward rather than pressing down. A roti that is too thick does not blister and flex properly; too thin and the filling tears through. Aim for about 3 mm.
Cook and grind the split peas

Boil the {{split-peas-yellow}} in plenty of water with the {{garlic}} for 30 to 35 minutes until completely tender but not yet dissolving to mush. Drain very well and spread on a tray to steam dry for 10 minutes. Transfer to a food processor with the {{cumin-seeds}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} if using, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Pulse to a coarse dry paste — not smooth. The filling should hold its shape when pressed. Cool completely.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add the {{vegetable-oil}} and rub into the flour. Add enough warm water (about 180 ml) to bring the dough together into a soft, smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Cover and rest for 30 minutes. Divide into 6 balls. Flatten each ball, place a heaped tablespoon of the split pea filling in the centre, gather the edges of the dough over the filling, and press closed. Rest 5 minutes.

On a lightly floured surface, roll each filled ball gently into a round about 25 cm across and 3 mm thick. Heat a flat pan or tawa over medium-high heat. Cook each roti for 1 to 2 minutes per side, brushing with {{vegetable-oil}}, until blistered and cooked through. The roti should be soft and pliable. Stack cooked roti and cover with a cloth to keep warm and soft.

items.symptom: Filling tears through the dough when rolling
items.cause: Filling too wet or dough rolled too thin
items.fix: Ensure the split peas are dry before filling. Roll gently from the centre outward rather than pressing

items.symptom: Roti hard and stiff after cooking
items.cause: Cooked over too high heat or not covered after cooking
items.fix: Stack cooked roti immediately and cover — the steam from the hot roti keeps them soft. Medium-high heat is correct; very high heat sets the outside before the inside cooks through

items.cause: Filling not spread evenly when stuffing
items.fix: When rolling, use gentle pressure to guide the filling toward the edges. Accept that some variation is normal

content.text: Dhalpuri roti is the highest expression of Trinidadian roti-making. It requires more skill than sada roti (the plain version) but the resulting flatbread is more complex and satisfying. In Trinidad it is made at home for special occasions and at roti shops that serve it as a wrap around curry, channa, or stewed meats. The roti shop is as central to Trinidadian food culture as the doubles vendor — a place where you eat standing up, quickly, the roti wrapped around a filling and handed over in wax paper.
